planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; plant two or more different clones together for better berry set, as cross-pollination improves fruit production.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to partial shade; arrowwood viburnum is one of the most ecologically valuable native shrubs in eastern North America, supporting over 100 species of Lepidoptera larvae and providing abundant flat-topped white flower clusters in late spring that supply nectar to specialist native bees, followed by clusters of dark blue-black berries in late summer and fall that provide critical pre-migration food for dozens of songbird species. The straight, flexible stems were historically used by Native peoples for arrow shafts. Outstanding fall color ranging from yellow to glossy red-purple. Extremely adaptable to soil types, pH ranges, and light conditions. A foundational native shrub for any wildlife-supporting landscape.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in any moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil; nannyberry is one of the few fruiting native shrubs that performs in full shade. Plant two or more for best berry production.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to full shade; nannyberry is the most shade-tolerant native viburnum and one of the only large fruiting shrubs or small trees that produces a meaningful crop of edible berries in deep shade. The clustered blue-black berries ripen in August–September and are sweet and raisin-like when fully ripe — genuinely pleasant fresh eating, unlike most native berries. Songbirds and bears consume the fruit voraciously. The plant can develop into a multi-stemmed small tree 15–20 feet tall with arching branches; allow space for its natural form or remove lower stems to develop a tree-like habit. Excellent for naturalizing in difficult shaded sites under mature tree canopy where most other edibles fail completely.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring or fall in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; the plant naturalizes by suckering to form attractive colonies along stream banks and shaded slopes.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, deeply acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; drooping leucothoe is a graceful native evergreen shrub of mountain stream banks and ravines in the Southern Appalachians, with long arching stems clothed in lustrous lance-shaped leaves that turn bronzy-burgundy-purple in winter — one of the finest winter foliage colors in the shade garden. Tiny white bell-shaped flowers in May are fragrant and nectar-rich for early bees. The plant spreads by stolons to form a layered, naturalistic colony that suppresses weeds effectively. The cultivar 'Rainbow' has cream-pink-green variegated foliage; 'Girard's Rainbow' and 'Zeblid' (Scarletta) are also widely available. All parts are toxic. Excellent massed under rhododendrons, azaleas, or as an understory layer beneath oaks.

planting.note: Plant container shrubs in spring in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; keep crown at or above soil level. Do not allow to dry out during establishment.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained soil in partial to full shade; plumleaf azalea holds a unique distinction among native deciduous azaleas — it blooms in July and August, later than every other native azalea species, and is therefore in bloom when every other azalea in the garden has long finished. The large, brilliant orange-red (rarely yellow) flowers appear on the shrub in midsummer, providing color and hummingbird nectar in a season when the woodland garden often lacks bloom. This is one of the rarest native azaleas in the wild, found only in southwestern Georgia and eastern Alabama, but it is a superb and underused garden plant. It tolerates the heat and partial shade of zone 8–9 summers better than most native azaleas. Mulch heavily with pine bark to maintain soil moisture and acidity.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in very early spring in wet, mucky, slightly acidic soil at the edge of a pond, stream, or bog garden; the plant requires consistently wet conditions and will not survive dry summer soils.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in wet to saturated, mucky, slightly acidic soil at the margin of a pond, stream, or bog garden in partial shade to full sun; marsh marigold is one of the earliest spring wildflowers in cold-climate wetlands, producing brilliant golden-yellow waxy flowers like large buttercups in March and April — sometimes pushing through the last snow at the edge of a thawing stream. It is the quintessential cold-climate bog and rain garden plant, growing naturally in springs, bogs, and stream banks from Labrador to North Carolina. All parts are mildly toxic when raw but the flower buds have been used as a caper substitute when pickled. The plant goes completely dormant in summer heat; interplant with other wetland perennials to fill the gap. Not suited to zone 8 and south where summers are too hot.
